name: Florida Affirmative Defenses & Defensive Motions index
note: Original index of Florida affirmative defenses and threshold/defensive doctrines, authored from public Florida law (Fla. R. Civ. P. 1.110(d) & 1.140(b), the Florida Statutes, and case law). Each defense lists what the defendant must show, who bears the BURDEN (defendant unless noted), the authority, and the claim types it commonly defeats. Affirmative defenses must be pleaded (1.110(d)) or are waived. 'cat': procedural | contract | tort | equitable | statutory | constitutional. VERIFY each against current law.
rule_note: Fla. R. Civ. P. 1.110(d) enumerated affirmative defenses: accord and satisfaction, arbitration and award, assumption of risk, comparative negligence, discharge in bankruptcy, duress, estoppel, failure of consideration, fraud, illegality, injury by fellow servant, laches, license, payment, release, res judicata, statute of frauds, statute of limitations, waiver — and any other matter constituting an avoidance or affirmative defense. Rule 1.140(b) threshold defenses (MTD): lack of subject-matter / personal jurisdiction, improper venue, insufficiency of process / service, failure to state a cause of action, failure to join indispensable parties.

show: the claim accrued more than the limitations period before suit (§ 95.11)
accrual not delayed by the discovery rule / tolling
the absolute outer period ran (e.g., products § 95.031(2); construction/design § 95.11(3)(c) 10-yr from completion)
runs regardless of discovery

defenses.d: Failure to State a Cause of Action
show: the complaint, on its four corners, omits an essential element of the claim
the court has no power over the case type/amount (e.g., county vs. circuit threshold)
no long-arm act (§ 48.193) AND/OR insufficient minimum contacts (Venetian Salami two-step)
defenses.burden: plaintiff pleads basis; defendant contests by affidavit

show: venue is not where the cause accrued / property lies / defendant resides (Ch. 47)

defenses.d: Insufficiency of Process / Service of Process
show: the summons/complaint or the manner of service did not comply with Ch. 48/49

defenses.d: Failure to Join an Indispensable Party
show: a party whose interest is so connected that complete relief is impossible without it (e.g., a cotenant, a record lienholder)
plaintiff suffered no injury / is not the real party in interest (e.g., not the note holder; not the PR for wrongful death)
a prior final judgment on the merits
same parties/privies
same cause of action (identity of thing sued for, cause, persons, quality)
the identical issue was actually litigated and decided
necessary to the prior judgment
mutuality of parties (Florida)
an earlier action between the same parties on the same subject is pending
a bona fide dispute
an agreement to settle it
performance of the new agreement (often a 'payment in full' check cashed)
the obligation was paid/discharged (proof of payment)
a valid release/settlement of the claim, supported by consideration
a valid new contract
that extinguished and replaced the old obligation
mutual assent of all parties
the voluntary, intentional relinquishment of a known right (by word or conduct)
a representation contrary to a later position
reliance
a detrimental change of position
defenses.defeats: a claim inconsistent with the representation

show: the bargained-for consideration failed/was not given
the contract is within § 725.01 (land, > 1 year, guaranty, etc.) and not in a signed writing
the contract's object or performance is illegal/against public policy
a wrongful act/threat
that overcame the party's free will
inducing the agreement
a false material statement
scienter
reliance inducing the contract
(mutual) both parties mistaken about a basic assumption; (unilateral) one party's mistake + inequity/knowledge
the plaintiff's own inequitable conduct directly related to the claim
defenses.defeats: any EQUITABLE claim (specific performance, injunction, quiet title)

show: an unreasonable delay in asserting the right
with knowledge
prejudice to the defendant
defenses.defeats: equitable claims (where SOL not strictly applicable)

show: money damages would fully compensate the plaintiff
defenses.defeats: injunction, specific performance, accounting, constructive trust

show: the plaintiff pursued an inconsistent remedy to conclusion (e.g., affirmed the contract, then sued to rescind)
(recoupment) a claim arising from the same transaction reducing the recovery; (set-off) a mutual debt from a different transaction
the plaintiff's own negligence contributed to the harm
(2023) plaintiff > 50% at fault BARS recovery in negligence
the plaintiff knew of and voluntarily exposed itself to a specific risk (express, or limited implied — contact sports)
the only loss is to the product itself / the subject of the contract
no independent tort duty or personal injury/other-property damage
defenses.defeats: tort claims duplicating a contract (products line; Tiara narrowed it)

show: no physical impact and no recognized exception (e.g., not in the zone of danger / no IIED/defamation exception)
(absolute) statements in judicial/legislative proceedings; (qualified) good-faith on a subject of common interest, not abused by malice
the gist of the statement is true
the plaintiff consented to the conduct (contact, entry, use of likeness)
a reasonable belief of imminent harm and proportional force
legitimate competition or a financial/contractual interest justified the interference
probable cause for the prior proceeding, or full disclosure to and reliance on counsel
the debt was discharged in the defendant's bankruptcy (and is not a § 523 nondischargeable debt)
a governmental defendant + no § 768.28 waiver / the planning-level or discretionary-function bar / caps ($200k/$300k) + presuit notice not met
an employer/co-employee + a work injury within § 440 + no intentional-tort/gross-negligence exception

defenses.d: Arbitration and Award / Agreement to Arbitrate
show: a valid arbitration agreement covering the dispute (motion to compel/stay)
defenses.defeats: litigation of an arbitrable claim (stays it)

show: the plaintiff failed to take reasonable steps to reduce its damages
a license was required for the work
the plaintiff was unlicensed when contracting/performing
defenses.defeats: contract AND quantum-meruit recovery by an unlicensed contractor

show: a valid express contract governs the same subject matter
the subcontract clearly and unambiguously conditions payment on the GC's receipt of payment from the owner
the owner has not paid
defenses.defeats: a subcontractor's payment claim vs. the GC (until owner pays)

defenses.d: Owner Paid the GC (to sub's UE)
show: the owner already paid the general contractor in full for the work
defenses.defeats: a subcontractor's unjust-enrichment claim vs. the owner

show: the violation was unintentional and resulted from a bona fide error despite procedures to avoid it
the practice/actor is exempt under § 501.212 (regulated industry, expressly permitted conduct)

defenses.d: Good-Faith Transferee for Reasonably Equivalent Value (FUFTA § 726.109)
show: the transferee took the asset in good faith
and gave reasonably equivalent value
(or is a protected subsequent transferee)
defenses.defeats: FUFTA actual-intent avoidance (§ 726.105(1)(a)); limits recovery; yields a retained lien to the extent of value given

show: the FUFTA action was filed > 4 years after the transfer (constructive), or > 4 yr AND > 1 yr after discovery (actual intent), or > 1 yr (§ 726.106(2) insider)
the debtor received reasonably equivalent value in exchange for the transfer (e.g., dollar-for-dollar, a genuine sale, satisfaction of an antecedent debt)
defenses.defeats: constructive fraudulent transfer (§ 726.105(1)(b), § 726.106(1)) and rebuts a badge under § 726.105(2)(h)
